name: El Chaparro de la Vega y Peñón de Zaframagón — round trip Vía Verde de la Sierra
name_original: El Chaparro de la Vega y Peñón de Zaframagón — ida y vuelta Vía Verde de la Sierra
region_intro: Jogging around Coripe offers diverse terrain, from converted railway lines to mountainous paths within the Sierra de Algodonales. The region features the Vía Verde de la Sierra, a relatively flat route traversing tunnels and viaducts, alongside more challenging trails in the surrounding hills and valleys. Natural features include the Peñón de Zaframagón Natural Reserve and ancient holm oak forests. This area provides a variety of running experiences for different fitness levels. Best jogging routes around Coripe The most popular jogging route is El Chaparro de la Vega y Peñón de Zaframagón — round trip Vía Verde de la Sierra, a 9.3 miles (14.9 km) trail that takes 1 hour 37 minutes to complete. This route follows a section of the Vía Verde, offering views of the Peñón de Zaframagón and passing by the ancient Chaparro de la Vega. Another top favourite among local runners is Sierra de Líjar Loop, a difficult 13.8 miles (22.2 km) path. This route explores the more mountainous terrain of the Sierra de Algodonales, providing a challenging experience with significant elevation changes. Local runners also love the El Chaparro de la Vega – Coripe loop from Coripe, a 10.7 miles (17.3 km) trail leading through holm oak forests and rolling hills, often completed in about 2 hours 20 minutes. Jogging around Coripe is defined by the Vía Verde de la Sierra, the mountainous Sierra de Algodonales, and ancient holm oak forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from accessible paths to more demanding trails. The routes in Coripe are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.1 stars from 7 reviews. More than 400 runners have used komoot to explore Coripe's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on running routes around Coripe?
A: Jogging routes in Coripe offer a diverse range of terrain. You'll find relatively flat and accessible paths along the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1043791" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt;, which follows an old railway line with compact ground and some tarmac sections. For a more challenging experience, the surrounding &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012700609/sierra-de-lijar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Algodonales&lt;/a&gt; provides mountainous paths with significant elevation changes, featuring hills, valleys, and traditional agricultural land like olive groves.

Q: Are there any easy running routes suitable for beginners or a relaxed jog?
A: While the guide primarily features moderate to difficult routes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1043791" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt; offers sections that are generally flat and accessible, making them suitable for an easier jog. These paths are ideal for those looking for a less strenuous experience amidst beautiful natural landscapes.

Q: What natural attractions or landmarks can I see while jogging in Coripe?
A: Coripe's running routes are rich with natural and historical sights. You can enjoy views of the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3219631" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñón de Zaframagón Natural Reserve&lt;/a&gt;, home to a large colony of griffon vultures, especially along the Vía Verde. Another notable landmark is the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3358115" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Chaparro de la Vega&lt;/a&gt;, a holm oak over 700 years old, which is a Natural Monument of Andalusia. The Vía Verde itself features numerous tunnels and viaducts, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3795122" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zaframagón Viaduct&lt;/a&gt;, offering unique scenic points.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the Coripe area?
A: Yes, there are several circular running routes available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40367053" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Chaparro de la Vega – Coripe loop from Coripe&lt;/a&gt; is a popular 10.7-mile trail that takes you through holm oak forests and rolling hills, starting and ending in Coripe.

Q: What do other runners say about the jogging routes in Coripe?
A: The running routes in Coripe are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.1 stars from 7 reviews. Runners often praise the varied terrain, from the accessible Vía Verde to the challenging mountain paths, and the stunning natural beauty, including views of the Peñón de Zaframagón and ancient holm oak forests.

Q: Can I find routes that offer scenic viewpoints in Coripe?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in Coripe offer fantastic scenic viewpoints. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012738869/el-chaparro-de-la-vega-y-penon-de-zaframagon-round-trip-via-verde-de-la-sierra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Chaparro de la Vega y Peñón de Zaframagón — round trip Vía Verde de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt; provides excellent views of the Peñón de Zaframagón and the Estrechón Canyon. Additionally, the mountainous trails in the Sierra de Algodonales, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38253207" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador del Dornajo loop from Algodonales&lt;/a&gt;, often lead to elevated spots with panoramic vistas of the surrounding landscape.

Q: Are there any running trails near Coripe that are not part of the Vía Verde de la Sierra?
A: Yes, beyond the Vía Verde, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012700609/sierra-de-lijar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Líjar Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a more challenging experience in the mountainous terrain of the Sierra de Algodonales. There are also routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14590915" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from Algodonales&lt;/a&gt;, which explores different parts of the region, providing varied landscapes and elevations.

Q: What is the best time of year for jogging in Coripe?
A: Coripe's Mediterranean climate makes spring and autumn particularly pleasant for jogging, with milder temperatures. During these seasons, the natural landscapes are vibrant, and the weather is generally comfortable for outdoor activities. Summer can be very hot, so early mornings or late evenings are recommended if running during this time. Winter offers cooler temperatures, which can be ideal for longer runs, though some higher elevation trails might be more exposed.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to eat near the running routes in Coripe?
A: Yes, especially along the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1043791" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3221171" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hostal Restaurante Estación de Coripe&lt;/a&gt; is located directly on the Vía Verde, offering a convenient spot for refreshments or a meal before or after your run. The town of Coripe itself also provides local amenities.

Q: How many running routes are available around Coripe?
A: There are over 25 running routes available around Coripe, catering to various fitness levels. These include a mix of moderate and difficult trails, ensuring there's something for every runner looking to explore the diverse landscapes of the Sierra de Algodonales and the Vía Verde de la Sierra.

Q: Is public transport available to access the running trails in Coripe?
A: While specific public transport details for direct trailheads can vary, Coripe is a small town, and local bus services might connect it to larger towns. For accessing the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1043791" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt;, the old railway station in Coripe serves as a key access point, which is generally reachable within the town.

Q: Are there any long-distance running trails in the Coripe area?
A: Yes, for those seeking longer distances, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1043791" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt; itself extends for 36 kilometers, offering a continuous path suitable for long-distance running. Additionally, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012700609/sierra-de-lijar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Líjar Loop&lt;/a&gt;, at 13.8 miles (22.2 km), provide a significant challenge with considerable elevation, ideal for endurance training.
tips: El Chaparro de la Vega is a large oak specimen over 200 years old, in the municipality of Coripe and around the Sierra Sur de Sevilla. Under its shadow, various playful and festive activities take place, among which the Fátima Pilgrimage in mid-May stands out.

A 135-meter-long viaduct, built using rectangular truncated pyramidal pilasters with mortared ashlars, is formed by the repetition of seven identical arches.
It was built to span the Guadalporcún River crossing before it meets the Guadamanil River. The latter originates in the Sierra de la Lebrona mountain range in the municipality of Pruna (Seville) and flows into the Guadalporcún River just a few meters from the viaduct. The confluence of the rivers has created a wide floodplain.
From this railway infrastructure, there is a privileged view of the Estrechón Canyon, a natural passage carved by the Guadalporcún River to traverse the foothills of the Zaframagón Rock. Just below, on the left bank of the Guadalporcún, we find the ruined building of what was once the Zaframagón mill. A little further west, other ruins can be seen. It is a small town built for workers on the railway line construction site.
Beautiful Greenway that winds through different "hills" until reaching the Peñón Zaframagón Natural Reserve. The gravel track is easy and there are many areas of shade for the hottest days!
